title: Shapes of molecules
summary: 9701 AS VSEPR theory — molecular shapes, bond angles, and lone pair effects. PhET Molecule Shapes simulation with step-synced guidance.
learningObjectives: Apply VSEPR to predict shapes from electron pair repulsion.
State bond angles for linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, and bent geometries.
Explain how lone pairs reduce bond angles below ideal values.
Relate shape to polarity and intermolecular forces.

simpleExplanation.title: Electron pairs repel
simpleExplanation.summary: VSEPR says valence electron pairs arrange to minimise repulsion. Count bonding pairs and lone pairs to name the shape and estimate bond angles.
steps: Count bonding pairs (shared pairs) and lone pairs on the central atom.
Arrange electron pairs to maximise separation — lone pairs repel more than bonding pairs.
Name the shape from the positions of atoms only (lone pairs are invisible in the name).
Use bond angle data as evidence — e.g. H₂O is bent (~104.5°) not linear because of 2 lone pairs.

flashcards.front: Shape with 2 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs?
Shape with 4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs?

flashcards.back: Pyramidal — 3 bonding pairs, 1 lone pair; angle ≈ 107°.

sections.content: **VSEPR** is a high-frequency 9701 topic — often 4–6 marks on Paper 2. Examiners want **named shapes**, **estimated bond angles**, and an explanation referencing **lone pair repulsion**.

items: 2 bp + 2 lp → bent (~104.5°); 5 bp → trigonal bipyramidal
Double bonds count as one electron pair domain in VSEPR

sections.question: Predict the shape and bond angle of SF₄ (S has 6 valence electrons; forms 4 S–F bonds).
sections.solution:
4 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair on S → **see-saw** (based on trigonal bipyramidal).

Lone pair compresses equatorial F–S–F angles below 120° / 90° ideal values.

sections.content: Draw a **dot-and-cross** or **electron pair count** before naming the shape. Cambridge frequently tests **ClF₃**, **SF₆**, and **PF₅** — practise counting lone pairs on the central atom.

sections.label: Mark a shapes of molecules question
